text:
INDIANAPOLIS (WISH) — The National Waste & Recycling Foundation and the National Waste & Recycling Association launched a national public service campaign in January 2026 aimed at reducing battery-related fires in the waste and recycling industry.

The initiative, titled “Skip the Bin – Turn Your Batteries In!,” encourages residents to properly dispose of lithium-ion batteries and is designed to protect sanitation workers, waste facilities and surrounding communities.

The campaign follows a surge in fires linked to discarded batteries. Industry officials reported more than 1,000 battery-related fires nationwide in 2025 at locations including collection trucks, transfer stations, recycling facilities and landfills.

The effort comes as Indiana emerges as one of the fastest-growing manufacturing states for electric vehicle batteries.

Michael E. Hoffman, president of the National Waste & Recycling Foundation and CEO of the National Waste & Recycling Association, said many households now contain numerous lithium-ion batteries but residents often lack information about how to safely discard them.

“There were over a thousand fires in 2025,” Hoffman said. “There’s basically one every single day in every state in just the waste and recycling infrastructure, whether it’s collection, transfer, the recycling facility, or at the landfill, or a waste energy plant. We have a fire every day.”

The campaign warns that lithium-ion batteries operate through chemical reactions rather than simple electrical storage. When damaged or improperly handled, they can undergo thermal runaway — a rapid reaction that can cause temperatures to rise to several thousand degrees Fahrenheit within milliseconds. These fires cannot be extinguished with water, creating significant hazards in homes and waste facilities.

The foundation partnered with the U.S. Forest Service to feature the agency’s mascot, Woodsy Owl, in the campaign — one of the character’s most prominent public appearances in about two decades.

Hoffman said the Forest Service joined the initiative because of concerns about campers discarding batteries into campfires in national forests.

“We went to the U.S. Forest Service knowing that Woodsy had been around for almost 54 years at that point,” Hoffman said. “The Forest Service has concerns about fires in the national forest. So they were thrilled with the idea that Woodsy could get behind something like this.”

In addition to fire safety, the campaign highlights the importance of recycling batteries to recover valuable materials. Lithium-ion batteries contain rare earth metals that can be difficult to obtain. Hoffman said about 60% to 70% of these materials can be recovered and reused to manufacture new batteries.

“These are rare earth precious metals, particularly in the lithium-ion batteries,” Hoffman said. “And these are hard to find rare earth metals.”

The campaign’s website, Batterysafetynow.org, includes a ZIP code locator to help residents find nearby drop-off locations. About 20,000 retail sites nationwide participate in the program, including Home Depot, Lowe’s, Walmart, Best Buy, Target, Tractor Supply Company and Staples.

“You put your zip code in and there are 20,000 retail locations around the country, one pretty much within 10 miles of most zip codes, not all, but most,” Hoffman said.

The program promotes a three-step disposal process: Prepare, Locate and Deliver. Residents are advised to tape the ends of batteries to prevent contact, place them in a resealable bag or box and bring them to a participating retailer.

Once collected, the Battery Network coordinates transportation to processing depots, where batteries are sorted by chemical composition and prepared for recycling.

Hoffman said the foundation hopes to expand the program by partnering with grocery stores.

“Our next big objective is, can we get the grocery industry to agree to let us do this in grocery stores as well?” Hoffman said. “And then we’re pretty much wrapped around the consumer almost everywhere you might go.”

name: IRA Tax Credits
title: First Solar: Tax Credit Dependency Masking Backlog Depletion - GuruFocus
description:
Being vertically integrated with U.S. manufacturing capacity meant First Solar qualified for every tax credit and domestic content bonus Washington handed out.
At current run rates (5+ GW per quarter), the 54.5 GW backlog represents about 2.5-3 years of production.
Back in 2020 when solar was booming, First Solar added 11.9 GW in bookings against a 19.5 GW backlog—a 61% refresh rate.
As of Q3 2025 that figure had collapsed to 2.7 GW added against 54.5 GW backlog—a 5% refresh rate.
At 2.4-2.5 GW average quarterly shipments (conservative given recent rates are higher), the current 54.5 GW backlog gets depleted in 5-6 years.
text:
Investment Thesis

First Solar's recent Q4 2025 earnings report confirmed exactly what bears have been warning about: the backlog is drying up, and the near-term growth narrative has hit a wall. Management shocked the market in late February by issuing 2026 revenue guidance of just $4.9 to $5.2 billion—missing Wall Street estimates by roughly $1 billion and implying flat-to-negative top-line growth. The stock's subsequent plunge shows the market is finally being forced to wake up to the fundamental math problem underlying this business.

First Solar has separated itself from the solar industry carnage that consumed peers like Enphase and SolarEdge. The company actually manufactures modules profitably and operates at scale—competitive advantages that kept it alive when others collapsed.

The core problem is simple math. Section 45X tax credits contribute $0.17 per watt to First Solar's economics—about 55% of the $0.30/watt selling price. These credits phase out starting in 2030 and disappear entirely by 2033. Strip them out and First Solar's ~40% gross margins collapse to around 7-10%. The company reported a subsidized EPS of $14.21 in 2025, but without credits, that drops to maybe $2-3. At current prices around $192 per share, you're paying 64-96x normalized earnings for a business whose competitive advantage expires on a known schedule.

The backlog depletion makes this worse. Contracted orders have fallen from 78.3 GW at end-2023 down to just 50.1 GW by the end of 2025—a massive drop while the company shipped 17.5 GW last year. Even worse, due to heavy contract cancellations, First Solar actually posted negative 0.9 GW in net bookings for the full year 2025. At current booking rates against 4-5 GW quarterly shipments, First Solar hits an order cliff by 2028, right when tax credits start phasing out. Manufacturing issues with Series 7 modules and rising input costs reinforce the problem but aren't the primary thesis.

Five-Year Performance: The Last Survivor

Over the past five years FSLR has delivered nearly 200% returns, massively outperforming solar peers. The comparison is stark when you look at what happened to everyone else in the sector. Canadian Solar (CSIQ) is down considerably from its peaks despite using the same crystalline silicon technology as Chinese manufacturers. The residential/commercial equipment players got destroyed—Enphase (ENPH) down 89%, SolarEdge (SEDG) down 77%.

First Solar avoided this fate because it actually had a real business. The company was generating $3 billion in annual revenue back in 2019 when most of these others were still burning cash trying to scale. Being vertically integrated with U.S. manufacturing capacity meant First Solar qualified for every tax credit and domestic content bonus Washington handed out. When the Inflation Reduction Act passed in 2022, First Solar was positioned to capture maximum benefit while competitors scrambled.

Shares did nosedive in late 2024—dropping from $280s to the $160s—which coincided with the company's backlog starting to deplete rapidly. he brief late-2024 rebound back above $200 seemed driven by renewed optimism about solar demand and AI data center power needs. I'm not buying it. The fundamental problems with First Solar's business model haven't changed.

The CdTe Advantage: Real But Overstated

First Solar manufactures thin-film modules using Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) technology instead of crystalline silicon. This gives the company some genuine technical advantages, particularly in utility-scale applications. CdTe modules handle heat better than silicon—performance doesn't degrade as much in high temperatures. They're also cheaper to install because they're frameless and can be "sandwiched" into mounting systems without the labor-intensive racking that silicon modules require.

The company operates fully integrated manufacturing across five locations: Ohio, Alabama, Louisiana, Vietnam, and Malaysia. Vertical integration means First Solar controls the entire supply chain from raw materials through finished modules. This is a big part of why gross margins run 15-20 percentage points higher than crystalline silicon manufacturers—First Solar isn't dealing with third-party suppliers at multiple steps capturing margin.

But there are trade-offs. Crystalline silicon still beats CdTe on pure efficiency—22% vs. 19-20%—and performs better at high altitudes. First Solar's Series 7 modules represent the company's best technology, incorporating bifacial capability to capture reflected light. Series 6, the older generation, is being phased down with 1 GW of capacity cuts in Malaysia and Vietnam announced for 2025.

The manufacturing process itself is straightforward: buy soda-lime float glass substrate, apply transparent conductive oxide, heat and sublimate cadmium and tellurium in a vacuum with nitrogen gas to form thin film, then laser cut to create cells. Four main input costs: glass, cadmium, tellurium, and nitrogen.

The technology advantages are real, but they're not sufficient to offset the structural issues in First Solar's business model. CdTe competes well against crystalline silicon when you include federal subsidies and domestic content bonuses. Without those policy tailwinds, the economics get challenging fast.

Backlog Collapse: The Data Tells An Ugly Story

End of 2023 the backlog stood at 78.3 GW. By end of 2024 that had dropped to 68.5 GW. By the end of 2025, the backlog had plummeted to just 50.1 GW (valued at $15 billion). That's a 28.2 GW decline in exactly two years.

During 2025, First Solar shipped a record 17.5 GW. However, the company recorded 8.3 GW of annual debookings (cancellations/breach of contracts) against only 7.4 GW of gross bookings. This means for the full year of 2025, net bookings were actually negative 0.9 GW.

During this same period First Solar shipped approximately 19.4 GW—14.1 GW in 2024 plus roughly 5.3 GW in Q3 2025 alone. The backlog declined by more than total shipments, meaning net bookings were actually negative when you account for cancellations.

Breaking down 2024's booking activity shows the problem:

Gross bookings to U.S. utility customers: 5.1 GW at $0.309/watt

India volume: 0.6 GW

Below-spec distributor sales: 0.1 GW

Total gross bookings: ~5.8 GW

Contract terminations: 2.4 GW

Net bookings: 4.4 GW against 14.1 GW shipped

First Solar is consuming backlog 3.2x faster than replenishing it. Management calls this a "highly selective approach to contracting" given the "long-dated time frame" they're selling into and "uncertainty related to the policy environment." Translation: demand isn't strong enough at acceptable prices, so we're walking away from deals that don't meet our margin targets.

Q3 2025 was rough—6.9 GW of total debookings including 6.6 GW from BP affiliates citing "defaults." Losing 12% of your entire backlog from one customer in a single quarter is not normal. Management provided zero detail on whether BP's issue was project economics, financing problems, or dissatisfaction with First Solar's product. Regardless, it raises serious questions about backlog quality.

At current run rates (5+ GW per quarter), the 54.5 GW backlog represents about 2.5-3 years of production. If new bookings continue at 1-2 GW per quarter—the trend over the past eight quarters—First Solar faces a visible order cliff by 2028. This timeline coincides perfectly with 45X tax credits beginning their phase-out in 2030.

I tracked the ratio of quarterly gross bookings to total backlog over five years. Back in 2020 when solar was booming, First Solar added 11.9 GW in bookings against a 19.5 GW backlog—a 61% refresh rate. As of Q3 2025 that figure had collapsed to 2.7 GW added against 54.5 GW backlog—a 5% refresh rate. The company is depleting its order book far faster than it can replenish, and the trend is accelerating in the wrong direction.

Production has also been exceeding shipments for multiple quarters. First Solar produced 3.6 GW in Q3 2025 but shipped 5.3 GW, drawing down finished goods inventory built during 2023-2024's capacity expansion. With 21 GW of nameplate capacity and actual production running around 16-17 GW annually, utilization sits at 75-80%. The new Louisiana facility adds another 3.5 GW of capacity in 2025-2026, further depressing utilization unless bookings accelerate dramatically.

Looking at inventory turnover, raw material days have been declining—meaning materials sit in inventory longer before being used in production. During 2022's demand surge this figure compressed as First Solar couldn't produce fast enough. Now it's expanding again, indicating softer demand conditions. f solar demand was as strong as the bulls suggest, you'd expect to see raw material days compressing, not expanding.

The math is pretty clear. At 2.4-2.5 GW average quarterly shipments (conservative given recent rates are higher), the current 54.5 GW backlog gets depleted in 5-6 years. Factor in that gross bookings have averaged just 1.6 GW quarterly over the past two years and the timeline compresses further. First Solar is racing toward an order book depletion that happens right as tax credits disappear.

The Tax Credit Math: $0.17/Watt Makes All The Difference

Section 45X advanced manufacturing production credits are not a minor benefit to First Solar's economics—they're the entire margin structure. Understanding the magnitude is essential to valuing this business correctly.

First Solar receives tax credits for producing solar components at U.S. facilities. The credits stack for vertically integrated production: $0.07/watt for modules, $0.04/watt for cells, plus additional credits for wafers and other components. Total credit value runs $0.12-0.17/watt depending on configuration.

In Q4 2024 First Solar recognized $396 million in government grants, which reduced cost of goods sold from $1.38 billion to $984 million—a 28.7% reduction in reported costs. On $1.58 billion in Q4 revenue this transformed:

Reported gross margin: 37.6%

Normalized gross margin (excluding grants): 12.7%

Annualizing that quarter's figures suggests roughly $1.6 billion in annual 45X benefits on $4.2 billion in revenue—38% of revenue funded by tax credits.

The per-watt economics make this crystal clear. First Solar's Q4 2024 average selling price was $0.305/watt. With tax credits contributing approximately $0.17/watt:

Selling price: $0.305/watt

Tax credit: $0.17/watt

Total economic realization: $0.475/watt

Production cost (normalized): ~$0.265/watt

Economic margin: $0.21/watt (44% gross margin)

Without tax credits:

Selling price: $0.305/watt

Production cost: ~$0.265/watt

Normalized margin: $0.04/watt (13% gross margin)

These normalized margins align with First Solar's historical pre-IRA performance during 2017-2021 when the company operated without significant manufacturing subsidies.

The 45X phase-out schedule is not speculation—it's written into law:

t $192 per share First Solar trades at roughly 13.5x trailing earnings. But those earnings assume full 45X benefits. On a normalized 2033+ earnings basis the multiple is 80-120x. The market is either assuming: (1) Congress extends 45X beyond 2032, (2) First Solar reduces costs 50%+ within seven years, or (3) some combination of both. Neither assumption shows up in analyst models or management guidance.

For First Solar to maintain current profitability without 45X credits, production costs must decline from ~$0.265/watt to ~$0.18/watt (a 32% reduction) while simultaneously defending $0.30/watt ASPs against crystalline silicon alternatives trading at $0.15-0.18/watt. Nothing in the company's cost structure, input cost trends, or technology roadmap suggests this is achievable within the seven-year timeline before credits disappear.

Manufacturing Issues and Capacity Expansion

First Solar isn't just facing demand-side challenges. The company disclosed manufacturing problems with Series 7 modules in Q4 2024, resulting in warranty reserves of $56-100 million. Management cited "higher-than-anticipated ramp-related charges" at the Alabama facility, which began production in 2024.

For a company with 44% gross margins and $4.2 billion in annual revenue, incurring $56-100 million in warranty costs (1.3-2.4% of revenue) indicates meaningful quality control issues. Series 7 is First Solar's flagship technology—the foundation for future copper replacement (CuRe) and perovskite innovations. If the company can't manufacture Series 7 reliably at volume, the entire technology roadmap becomes questionable.

The 1 GW capacity reduction in Malaysia and Vietnam announced for 2025 marks First Solar's first capacity pullback in years. Management cited "intense competition and unfavorable market conditions" in international markets. Series 6 modules produced internationally can't leverage domestic content bonuses and face direct competition from Chinese crystalline silicon at commodity pricing. The reduction suggests approximately 5% of total capacity is unprofitable without U.S. policy support.

Meanwhile First Solar is investing $1.1 billion in the Louisiana facility, which adds 3.5 GW of capacity and should reach full production by 2026. This brings total nameplate capacity to 25+ GW. The problem: with bookings running at 1-2 GW quarterly and backlog declining, First Solar is expanding capacity into weakening demand. Utilization already sits at 75-80%—the Louisiana ramp further depresses that figure unless bookings accelerate dramatically.

Can Costs Decline Fast Enough?

Bulls argue that scale economies and technology improvements will offset 45X phase-outs. This requires believing normalized production costs can decline from ~$0.265/watt today to ~$0.15-0.18/watt by 2032-2033 to maintain acceptable margins without subsidies.

That seems unlikely. As I mentioned earlier, cadmium and tellurium prices have risen 63-67% over five years. These are byproducts of zinc and copper mining—supply doesn't respond to solar demand. Glass and nitrogen costs are also up 32-34%. Unless global mining activity surges or First Solar develops alternative materials, input cost pressures persist or worsen.

First Solar's technology roadmap includes copper replacement (CuRe) for improved efficiency and reduced degradation, bifacial Series 7 for higher energy yield, and perovskite tandem cells targeting 25%+ efficiency. Even if all three succeed on schedule—optimistic given Series 7 manufacturing issues—the combined impact would be:

Efficiency improvement: +2-3% absolute (from ~19% to ~21-22%)

Energy yield improvement: +10-15% (bifacial plus degradation reduction)

Total LCOE improvement: ~15-20%

This allows First Solar to either maintain pricing while improving value proposition or reduce pricing proportionally. Either scenario generates maybe $0.02-0.03/watt in additional margin—far short of the $0.17/watt lost from 45X phase-outs.

The company has scaled from 6 GW capacity in 2020 to 21 GW in 2024 while maintaining $0.30/watt ASPs and 38-44% gross margins. Scale benefits have been captured in maintaining margins despite input cost inflation rather than reducing absolute costs. Going from 21 GW to 25 GW (a 19% increase) won't drive transformational cost reductions when utilization already sits at 67%.

Political Risk: Extensions Seem Unlikely

Could Congress extend 45X beyond 2032? Management will lobby hard for extensions or alternative support mechanisms, and they've been effective at Washington engagement historically.

But I'm skeptical. Federal deficits exceed $2 trillion annually with no fiscal discipline from either party. Defense, entitlements, and debt service crowd out discretionary spending. The recent "One Big Beautiful Bill" actually accelerated some renewable phase-outs for wind while maintaining the solar schedule—suggesting political momentum is toward reducing subsidies, not extending them. Getting 60 Senate votes or reconciliation to reverse the bipartisan 45X phase-out timeline seems implausible given the budget environment. First Solar's national security argument for domestic manufacturing might resonate, but extending credits through 2035-2040 (what's needed to support current valuations) is a low-probability outcome.

Valuation: Priced For Perfection

At ~$192 per share (a roughly $20.6 billion market cap), First Solar trades at ~13.5x trailing earnings assuming full 45X benefits. Tellingly, management refused to give explicit EPS guidance for 2026 on their recent call, citing "volatile tax and accounting effects." On normalized earnings without subsidies, the multiple sits at 64-96x. The stock prices in outcomes that don't appear achievable given the shrinking backlog, flat 2026 revenue guidance, and the legislative phase-out timeline.

If 45X phase-outs proceed as scheduled and costs don't decline meaningfully, 2032-2033 earnings drop to $2-3 per share. Apply a 15-20x multiple to that figure and you get $30-60 per share fair value—75-87% downside from current levels.

Even if you give First Solar credit for some cost reductions and assume earnings stabilize at $6-8 per share post-2032, fair value would be $90-120 per share at 15x—still 50-62% downside.

The only way current valuations make sense is if you believe either: (1) Congress extends 45X through 2035+, or (2) First Solar achieves breakthrough cost reductions that aren't visible in current data. Both scenarios require faith over evidence.

For investors seeking 15%+ annual returns, First Solar would need to reach $350+ within 24 months. That requires the low-probability scenario where bookings reaccelerate, costs decline dramatically, AND multiple expansion occurs. I don't see it happening.

What Changes The Thesis

Three developments would materially improve the investment case:

First, sustained booking acceleration to 6-8 GW quarterly at $0.31-0.32/watt ASPs. This would demonstrate genuine demand beyond subsidy-driven pull-forward and extend the backlog runway. Watch for announcements with creditworthy counterparties, specific project names/timelines, and pricing terms that include inflation escalators.

Second, evidence of meaningful cost reduction. If First Solar demonstrates production costs declining to $0.22-0.24/watt during 2025-2026—before 45X phase-outs begin—it would validate that scale and technology can offset subsidy reductions. This requires gross margins holding at 38-40% even as ASPs decline.

Third, legislative action on 45X extensions. If Congress passes legislation extending credits through 2035+ or implementing alternative domestic manufacturing support, it extends First Solar's economic moat and provides runway for cost reductions.

None of these catalysts seem imminent based on current data.

At what price does this get interesting? I'd start paying attention around $120-140. At that level you're pricing in maybe $5-6 per share in normalized earnings at a 20-25x multiple, which assumes some cost reduction success but doesn't require perfection. You get optionality on the upside if bookings reaccelerate or Congress extends credits, but you're not betting the farm on it.

The metrics I'm watching quarterly are pretty straightforward. Gross bookings need to trend above 4 GW consistently—ideally hitting 6 GW within three quarters. Cost per watt needs to show actual improvement, not just guidance. And capacity utilization matters—if it drops below 70% while backlog keeps shrinking, that's confirmation the demand problem is real.

The recent Q4 2025 earnings report made the 2028 backlog cliff glaringly visible. With the company posting negative net bookings for the full year and issuing flat revenue guidance for 2026, the market is finally being forced to price in this reality. On the flip side, if First Solar announces a major 8-10 GW contract with a creditworthy utility, I'd reassess immediately.

The Full Picture

First Solar operates an excellent business when subsidized by $0.17/watt in tax credits. The company's CdTe technology offers genuine advantages, vertical integration provides supply chain control, and management has executed capacity expansion competently through 2024.

The problem is valuation and timing. At $192 per share investors are paying over $20 billion for a business that generates $3-4 per share in sustainable earnings post-2032 unless costs decline dramatically or subsidies extend. That's 60-80x sustainable earnings for a mature manufacturer in a commoditizing industry.

The backlog depletion timeline (2028-2029) coincides almost perfectly with tax credit phase-outs (2030-2033). First Solar faces simultaneous demand-side challenges (order book running out) and margin compression (subsidies disappearing). This combination creates massive execution risk that the market is currently ignoring.

Current trends show booking rates of 1-2 GW quarterly against 5+ GW quarterly shipments—a 3:1 imbalance that's unsustainable. Input costs for cadmium, tellurium, and glass have risen 32-67% over five years while ASPs have declined 5%. Manufacturing issues with Series 7 and capacity reductions in Malaysia/Vietnam indicate operational challenges beyond just demand softness.

For a company that goes from 17x forward P/E to 115x normalized P/E without government grants, the risk/reward at $192 is unfavorable. The core issues—backlog depletion and tax credit dependency—haven't been addressed, and management's "selective contracting" approach is just a polite way of saying demand isn't strong enough at prices that generate acceptable margins.

I don't see a catalyst that changes this dynamic over the next 12-24 months. Until booking trends reverse materially or Congress signals willingness to extend 45X beyond 2032, First Solar looks overvalued even acknowledging its operational excellence relative to solar peers.

title: US energy chief defends waiver on Russian oil sanctions, blames fear for higher gas prices
description:
Appearing on multiple TV talk shows, Energy Secretary Chris Wright and ‌U.S.
Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz said a waiver issued last week to allow Indian purchases of Russian oil would alleviate pressure on the global market.
“We believe this is a small price to pay to get to a ‌world where energy prices are returned back to where they were,” Wright said on the “Fox News Sunday” program.
“But it won’t be,” Wright said, echoing President Donald Trump’s prediction that the war will last weeks rather than months.
Trump, in a Reuters interview on Thursday, predicted that gasoline prices will “drop very ⁠rapidly” when the war is ‌over.
text:
By Katharine Jackson and Curtis Williams

WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) – Trump administration officials on Sunday defended a decision to temporarily lift some sanctions on Russian oil and predicted that a sharp increase in gasoline prices resulting from the Iran war would ​last only weeks.

Appearing on multiple TV talk shows, Energy Secretary Chris Wright and ‌U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz said a waiver issued last week to allow Indian purchases of Russian oil would alleviate pressure on the global market.

“It’s a 30-day pause to allow, which is just kind of common sense, to allow the millions and millions of barrels of oil that are sitting out ‌on ​ships to go to Indian refineries,” Waltz said on NBC’s “Meet ⁠the Press.”

Wright told CNN’s “State of the ⁠Union” that the waiver can help “tamp this fear of shortage of oil, tamp the price spikes and the concerns we see in the marketplace.”

With the war now in its second week and no end in sight, Americans are grappling with higher prices at ​the pump, a new complicating factor for the U.S. economy, which unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs in February.

As of Friday, the national average price for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a gallon, ⁠up 11% from the previous week and the highest ⁠since September 2024, according to data from the motorists group AAA. ​Diesel was at $4.33, up 15% from a week ago, surging to the highest level since November ​2023.

“We believe this is a small price to pay to get to a ‌world where energy prices are returned back to where they were,” Wright said on the “Fox News Sunday” program.

There is no shortage of oil or natural gas, said Wright, who asserted that the price increases are based on “fear and perception” that the Iran operation will be a drawn-out ⁠affair.

“But it won’t be,” Wright said, echoing President Donald Trump’s prediction that the war will last weeks rather than months.

Trump, in a Reuters interview on Thursday, predicted that gasoline prices will “drop very ⁠rapidly” when the war is ‌over.

Senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, criticized energy speculators.

“The oil prices ⁠have gone up because you’ve got a bunch of oil traders ​out there ‌in their Gucci loafers, with their caramel Frappuccinos who are bidding ​up the price,” ⁠Kennedy said on “Fox News Sunday.”

Political analysts say a persistent rise in gasoline prices could hurt Republicans in the November midterm elections when control of the U.S. Congress will be at stake. A Reuters/Ipsos poll last month found that most respondents rejected Trump’s characterization of the economy as “booming.”

(Reporting by Katharine Jackson in Washington, Curtis Williams in Houston and Diana Jones in Chicago; Writing by Steve ​Holland; Editing by Matthew Lewis)

title: Lawler, Energy Secretary Chris Wright Visit Indian Point, Call for Rebuilding and Reopening ...
description:
Congressman Mike Lawler (NY-17) welcomed Department of Energy Secretary Chris Wright to the site of the former Indian Point Energy Center, where Lawler called for the plant to be rebuilt and reopened as part of an effort to lower energy costs and strengthen the reliability of New York’s electric grid.
Communities across the country, including those in New York, are currently serving as long-term nuclear waste storage sites after plant closures.
I want to thank Secretary Chris Wright for being here today and for his leadership on strengthening America’s energy security,” said Congressman Lawler.
“I’m calling for the rebuilding and reopening of Indian Point Energy Center and for an all-of-the-above energy strategy.
That means supporting nuclear energy, approving critical infrastructure like natural gas pipelines, and ensuring communities like Buchanan are not left behind after decades of helping power our state,” concluded Congressman Lawler.
text:
Congressman Mike Lawler (NY-17) welcomed Department of Energy Secretary Chris Wright to the site of the former Indian Point Energy Center, where Lawler called for the plant to be rebuilt and reopened as part of an effort to lower energy costs and strengthen the reliability of New York’s electric grid.

Standing at the former power plant site that once supplied roughly a quarter of the region’s electricity, the closure of Indian Point and the blocking of critical energy infrastructure have contributed to higher electricity costs and increased strain on New York’s electric grid.

During the visit, Rep. Lawler also highlighted the newly introduced Economic Recovery for Nuclear-Affected Communities Act, legislation designed to provide economic relief to communities that lost jobs and tax revenue after nuclear plant closures but continue to store spent nuclear fuel onsite.

Communities across the country, including those in New York, are currently serving as long-term nuclear waste storage sites after plant closures. While these communities lose jobs and critical tax revenues, the nuclear fuel remains onsite, limiting redevelopment and deterring future investments to the surrounding areas.

“New Yorkers are now paying nearly 60 percent more for electricity than the national average, and prices have risen dramatically over the past several years. You cannot shut down nuclear plants, block natural gas pipelines, and impose costly mandates, and then act surprised when families are hit with some of the highest utility bills in America. I want to thank Secretary Chris Wright for being here today and for his leadership on strengthening America’s energy security,” said Congressman Lawler.

“I’m calling for the rebuilding and reopening of Indian Point Energy Center and for an all-of-the-above energy strategy. That means supporting nuclear energy, approving critical infrastructure like natural gas pipelines, and ensuring communities like Buchanan are not left behind after decades of helping power our state,” concluded Congressman Lawler.

“Across the Northeast, including in New York, Americans are paying some of the highest electricity prices in the country because political leaders blocked critical infrastructure and prematurely shutdown power plants that deliver affordable, abundant power,” said Secretary Wright. “These actions have driven up electricity costs for millions of Americans. The Trump Administration believes energy policy should focus on the American people—not politics—by expanding reliable American energy, creating more American jobs, and lowering electricity prices for every American family and business across the country.”

The visit also reiterated the necessity of an all-of-the-above energy strategy that includes nuclear power, renewable energy, and responsibly developed natural gas to ensure energy remains reliable and affordable for families and businesses.

title: Champaign event 'plants the seed' for lifelong love of the environment | WCIA.com
description:
CHAMPAIGN, Ill. (WCIA) — Young social butterflies enjoyed the Monarch and Pollinator Fair in Champaign on Saturday.
The Prairie Group of the Illinois Chapter of the Sierra Club gave kids a chance to learn about butterflies, insects and seeds while also having fun at the McKinley Foundation.
The foundation’s Executive Director, Paula Hancock, said it’s never too early to learn about the part butterflies play in keeping a healthy ecosystem.
“I hope it sparks joy in learning about the environment and our insect friends and it could even encourage people who may became little scientists and environmentalists,” said Hancock.
“I think it’s never too early to plant that seed.” This is the fifth year the Prairie Group of the Illinois Chapter of the Sierra Club is teaching visitors about butterflies and sustainability.

title: Oil prices are soaring, but Trump is downplaying the need to tap the Strategic Petroleum Reserve
description:
Tapping the reserve is among the few things a president can do on his own to try to make an impact on oil prices.
Here is a look at what would be involved: What is the Strategic Petroleum Reserve?
Bush also authorized withdrawing nearly 34 million barrels during the Gulf War, although only 17 million barrels were used.
As the Iran war continues to escalate, oil prices have spiked rapidly, reaching their highest level since 2023.
And even if more oil were withdrawn from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, refineries could still be working with more expensive supply for a bit.
text:
NEW YORK (AP) — Oil prices have soared in the week since the U.S. and Israel launched their war against Iran, but President Donald Trump on Saturday downplayed the idea of turning to America’s Strategic Petroleum Reserve to ease the pressure.

Trump was asked by reporters on Air Force One about whether he would consider tapping the reserve. As the war continues to escalate across the Middle East, including in areas critical to the production and movement of oil and gas, that’s strained the energy sector globally. In the U.S., consumers are already facing higher gas prices, a key cost of living.

“We’ve got a lot of oil. Our country has a tremendous amount,” Trump said. “There’s a lot of oil out there. That’ll get healed very quickly.”

Trump’s Republican Party is under pressure over the issue of affordability ahead of November midterm elections. Tapping the reserve is among the few things a president can do on his own to try to make an impact on oil prices.

Here is a look at what would be involved:

What is the Strategic Petroleum Reserve?

The Strategic Petroleum Reserve is a collection of underground salt caverns in Texas and Louisiana that can hold more than 700 million barrels of oil, although it is not currently full. The reserve held more than 415 million barrels as of the end of last month, up from about 395 million barrels at this time in 2025, according to the U.S. Energy Department.

The reserve was created after the 1970s Arab oil embargo to give the United States a supply that could be used in an emergency. The amount of oil inside peaked more than a decade and a half ago, Energy Department data shows, when the reserve held more than 726.6 million barrels at one point.

How is it used?

Today, the U.S. exports more petroleum than it imports. But the reserve remains and has been tapped for various reasons over time, from offsetting the impact of hurricanes and ship-channel closings to raising money for deficit reduction.

Former presidents have turned to the reserve amid supply disruptions spanning from geopolitical conflicts, adding more supply onto the market in the hope of pushing prices lower. President Joe Biden drew significantly from the reserve in 2022 following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, dropping the stockpile to its lowest level since the 1980s. Back in 1991, President George H.W. Bush also authorized withdrawing nearly 34 million barrels during the Gulf War, although only 17 million barrels were used. And in 2011, President Barack Obama approved the release of 30 million barrels to offset the disruption of supply from Libya.

Why isn’t Trump tapping the reserve now?

As the Iran war continues to escalate, oil prices have spiked rapidly, reaching their highest level since 2023. The price for a barrel of Brent crude, the international standard, jumped 8.5% to $92.69 on Friday — up from nearly $70 a barrel just late last week. Meanwhile, benchmark U.S. crude climbed 12.2%, to $90.90 a barrel on Friday.

The Trump administration took one step last week to respond to the higher prices: the Treasury Department said India can buy crude oil and petroleum products from Russia until April 4, calling the sanctions waiver a “stop-gap measure” to “alleviate pressure” on the market.

But asked whether he would take other steps, such as tapping the SPR, Trump downplayed the need to use the supplies — noting the U.S. has “a tremendous amount” of oil — and instead focused on criticizing Biden, his predecessor, for drawing down the reserves.

Trump said he would begin filling up the SPR again at the “appropriate time, which is basically a gut instinct.”

Would gas get cheaper if the reserve was tapped?

Many factors go into prices at the pump.

In the U.S., the average price for gasoline has already climbed nationally — sitting at about $3.41 per gallon on Saturday, up about 43 cents a week ago, per motor club AAA. But refineries buy crude oil in advance, so it’s possible that the pain of higher prices could increase if the war drags on. And even if more oil were withdrawn from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, refineries could still be working with more expensive supply for a bit.

As always, some states also have pricier averages than others, due to factors ranging from nearby refinery supply to local fuel requirements and differing tax rates. On Saturday, California had the highest average of nearly $5.08 per gallon, while Kansas had the lowest of about $2.90 a gallon.

Gas prices are regressive — meaning lower-income people are more likely to spend a higher percentage of their money on fuel than affluent Americans. So increases hurt the most price-sensitive consumers.

How do they get the oil out?

Oil is lighter than water — that’s why disasters like those caused by the Exxon Valdez tanker and the Deepwater Horizon drilling rig create slicks on the surface. To remove oil from the reserves, water is pumped into the salt caverns, making the crude float to the surface, where it is captured and sent through pipelines to refineries.

___

AP writer Seung Min Kim contributed to this report from Washington.

title: Iran War Tests Whether Clean Tech Learned Any Lessons From 2022 - Bloomberg.com
authors: Alastair Marsh, Gautam Naik, Olivia Raimonde
description:
For green investors, the current war-fueled surge in oil and gas prices conjures up painful memories of 2022, when renewables were gripped by a selloff that lasted into 2025.
But at Jefferies, clients are being urged to double down on a bold upbeat call.
Last year, Jefferies started telling green investors they were entering the “glory days” of a strategy that had long seemed like a losing bet.
That advice ended up coinciding with a 44% surge in clean energy stocks in 2025, trouncing the 16% gain in the S&P 500 Index.

title: Galvanize raises $370 million for bet on decarbonization as a driver of real estate value
description:
Steyer’s climate investment firm, Galvanize Climate Solutions, launched a real estate strategy three years ago with a thesis that decarbonizing commercial buildings could boost asset value and net operating income.
Grey-to-green financing The Galvanize Real Estate Fund secured commitments from a diverse group of global pension funds, family offices, foundations, registered investment advisers, banks and other institutional investors.
The real estate fund has amassed a total of $1 billion, including debt financing it has lined up.
Galvanize has acquired 15 commercial properties, including 150 Milford Road, a 600,000-square-foot industrial complex in New Jersey (see, “Tom Steyer’s Galvanize makes third real estate grey-to-green deal”).
About 30% of Galvanize Real Estate Fund’s carried interest is dependent on meeting its impact goals.
text:
Tom Steyer sees an opportunity in America’s aging building stock. Steyer’s climate investment firm, Galvanize Climate Solutions, launched a real estate strategy three years ago with a thesis that decarbonizing commercial buildings could boost asset value and net operating income.

The strategy targets undercapitalized buildings in high-growth, supply-constrained markets that could benefit from solar installations, electrification of heating and cooling systems, and other energy-efficiency upgrades. The thesis looks prescient as a widening war in Iran drives up oil and gas prices.

“In an environment where the combined impact of rising electricity prices and market volatility is accelerating, there is a large and ongoing opportunity for the team to leverage decarbonization as a driver for value creation,” said Katie Hall, who co-founded Galvanize with Steyer after his brief presidential bid in 2020. The billionaire investor has now taken a leave from Galvanize to run for governor of California (see Steyer’s Q&A on ImpactAlpha).

Grey-to-green financing

The Galvanize Real Estate Fund secured commitments from a diverse group of global pension funds, family offices, foundations, registered investment advisers, banks and other institutional investors. Galvanize allocated $15 million of its own capital to the fund.

The real estate fund has amassed a total of $1 billion, including debt financing it has lined up.

Galvanize has acquired 15 commercial properties, including 150 Milford Road, a 600,000-square-foot industrial complex in New Jersey (see, “Tom Steyer’s Galvanize makes third real estate grey-to-green deal”). An in-house team of scientists, climate technologists and policy experts map out the decarbonization potential of the assets.

Galvanize aims to achieve portfolio-wide emissions reductions of 153% and avoid roughly 8,000 metric tons of emissions per year. About 30% of Galvanize Real Estate Fund’s carried interest is dependent on meeting its impact goals.

name: Detroit and Evs
title: America's EV Walkback Will Be Paid For A Decade From Now - Yahoo Autos
description:
American automakers are walking away from what may be the defining transportation revelation of our lifetimes: electrification.
Politically, socioeconomically, and in all other ways, young people got electric cars.
Even now, the cheapest American EV on the market, the Bolt, will only be around for a limited production rerun.
The gap between American cars and foreign ones will widen, and not just as a result of President Biden and Trump's protectionist trade policies.
American automakers may decide the investment is worth it, but they'll have to be pushed away from the easy money gas cars create for them first.
text:
Autoblog and Yahoo may earn commission from links in this article.

It's pretty easy to slap together 1,000 words of misty-eyed sighing over the way Things Used To Be In America, especially as it relates to our auto industry. It was, for a very long time, the best, only game in town. Those towns, by the way, prospered under American auto manufacturing. For now, though, it's best to power on by the usual riff on "Detroit used to be a city" or similar and discuss the future. American automakers are walking away from what may be the defining transportation revelation of our lifetimes: electrification. They will pay for it, and probably very soon.

EVs were (maybe) a huge swing and a miss in America

Back in the early 2010s, electric cars began to make a resurgence. A century past, these were in the mix some, before more efficient and powerful steam and gasoline engines won out. Things then seemed the inverse. New electric motors were more powerful and increasingly efficient than the rapidly outdated-looking gas engine.

Then, Tesla happened. The Model S is possibly as close as some of us will get to a Model T moment. Power, luxury, and status, the new EV had it all. Tesla forged on, and other automakers abroad began to develop their own EVs, from China to Europe. Less so at home in Detroit. GM, Ford, and the various brands that would become Stellantis waffled. Their EV efforts seemed a step behind for a few reasons: for one, these automakers seemed to think (and still do in some ways) that electric versions of gas cars are what buyers wanted. They didn't attempt to capture the magic early Teslas did, opting for a more traditional car experience. They also didn't make sense financially, and this alone pushed many young Americans out of the running.

These should have been the kinds of people automakers were targeting. Politically, socioeconomically, and in all other ways, young people got electric cars. But they couldn't afford them. Even now, the cheapest American EV on the market, the Bolt, will only be around for a limited production rerun.

Overseas, the story is and was different

While American automakers and audiences struggled with EVs, the rest of the world caught on to varying degrees. Norway is the classic example. The gold standard for EV adoption, an overwhelming majority of cars on the road in the nation are now fully electric. In China, consumers followed Tesla into electrification. A staggering number of nations now show a significant portion of sales from EVs, according to a report from The Verge: "20 percent in South Korea; 50 percent in China; 68 percent in Denmark; 65 percent in Sweden; and 96 percent in Norway."

What happened?

The gap in adoption rates, tech, and the generally poor state of the EV in America stems from a singular aspect: cash. Other nations flooded cash into EVs almost from the start. China might have collectively spent $250 billion on EV development and infrastructure. Despite the massive investment in transportation infrastructure as a result of the multi-billion-dollar Inflation Reduction Act, our electric infrastructure still lags behind that of many other nations.

That was, of course, before the reelection of President Trump. The Trump Administration has upended nearly every climate and emissions standard it can get its hands on, including those tied to EVs. The politicization of EVs at the hands of Trump and other political leaders from both parties has, no doubt, contributed culturally to the moment we find ourselves in now. Moreover, the gutting of federal emission standards gives automakers at home carte blanche to produce what they like without concern for the environment, and without concern for foreign competition. The gap between American cars and foreign ones will widen, and not just as a result of President Biden and Trump's protectionist trade policies.

What will happen?

For now, American automakers will continue to disclose that they've lost heaps of cash on EVs, like Ford did, killing the F-150 Lightning in favor of one with a gas engine tied to its electric motors the same day it announced a nearly $20 billion charge on its EV investments. Stellantis will put gasoline engines in everything that didn't already have one and roll back its hybrid lineups. GM will follow suit to some degree.

This makes America look like the last stand for the gasoline engine. Don't blame buyers, though, even though EV sales account for just 10% of the market. Left without affordable options, consistent infrastructure investments upheld across a handful of presidential administrations, and real, competitive options, it's easy to see why consumers turn to the more varied, more widely available gas market.

A decade from now, the bill will come due. Globally, EV investment will have paid off. Adoption rates continue to rise in most nations, and the Chinese market will gradually continue to expand outside its native borders. Eventually, that trade wall will likely come down, and the years of underinvestment and policy back-and-forth will create an American auto market that is hopelessly underprepared.

Some, like Ford, have acknowledged this. American automakers may decide the investment is worth it, but they'll have to be pushed away from the easy money gas cars create for them first.

name: Louisiana American Energy
title: Weaned off Putin's gas, Europe now addicted to US LNG
description:
At the same time, Europe has developed an unhealthy reliance on US liquefied natural gas (LNG).
Dubbed “freedom gas” following Russia’s invasion, the LNG is shipped in ever-higher volumes across the Atlantic via a network of new EU terminals.
By 2025, around 57% of LNG imports to the EU came from the US — around four times the 2021 levels.
These contracts include a deal by the newly established joint venture, Atlantic-See LNG, to supply US LNG through southeastern Europe, and potentially to Ukraine.
Atlantic-See LNG has already signed a 20-year deal with Venture Global, one of the biggest US suppliers of LNG to the EU.
text:
In 2021, Europe relied on Russia for around 50% of its natural gas, most delivered by pipeline. But following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Europe moved quickly to limit this dependency and slashed demand by two-thirds within three years.

At the same time, Europe has developed an unhealthy reliance on US liquefied natural gas (LNG). Dubbed “freedom gas” following Russia’s invasion, the LNG is shipped in ever-higher volumes across the Atlantic via a network of new EU terminals.

By 2025, around 57% of LNG imports to the EU came from the US — around four times the 2021 levels. This is expected to rise to up to 80% by 2030, according to analysis from the Institute for Energy Economics & Financial Analysis (IEEFA), a global team of energy finance analysts.

The US did not export any gas before 2016, as its reserves were kept for domestic consumption. It has since become the largest global supplier of LNG.

US gas dependence ‘contradicts’ energy diversification and transition goals

While gas will remain part of the EU’s energy transition, and most of Europe’s supply comes from Norway via pipelines, an exponential rise in US LNG imports could scuttle plans for energy independence and security, say analysts.

The IEEFA report argues that overreliance on American LNG “contradicts” the REPowerEU plan of 2022, which aims to end Europe’s dependence on Russian fossil fuels by “saving energy, diversifying supplies and accelerating the clean energy transition.”

The gas surge from across the Atlantic also belies the EU goal of making energy cheaper.

“US LNG is the most expensive for EU buyers, but European companies keep signing contracts,” said Ana Maria Jaller-Makarewicz, lead Europe energy analyst at the IEEFA and author of the report on the EU’s rising energy dependence.

These contracts include a deal by the newly established joint venture, Atlantic-See LNG, to supply US LNG through southeastern Europe, and potentially to Ukraine. The gas is to be shipped via Greek terminals through “Vertical Gas Corridor” pipelines in the region.

The Greek venture will meet with US officials on February 24 to secure up to 15 billion cubic meters of LNG annually for 20 years to supply central and eastern Europe, according to Reuters news agency. Atlantic-See LNG has already signed a 20-year deal with Venture Global, one of the biggest US suppliers of LNG to the EU.

Back in 2023, German state-owned business Securing Energy for Europe — formerly Gazprom Germania — signed a 20-year contract with Venture Global to import millions of tons of LNG a year from its Louisiana LNG plants.

EU dependence on US LNG growing

Venture Global CEO Mike Sabel said he was “grateful for the strong leadership of President Trump” and those “on both sides of the Atlantic who are encouraging further trade of American LNG.”

US President Donald Trump has championed his quest for global “energy dominance,” and in July 2025, negotiated a EU-US trade deal that commits Europe to buy $750 billion (€629 billion) of US energy (LNG, oil, and nuclear) annually by 2028.

“The US-EU energy deal has put pressure on Europe to buy more US energy,” said Jaller-Makarewicz of the agreement that, when formalized, will increase the risk of “high-risk geopolitical dependency” on American LNG.

In the words of the IEEFA report: “The deal effectively ties the EU’s energy supply to one seller, risking energy security and jeopardising gas reduction plans.”

Demand for US gas is also likely to increase when, in late 2027, the EU plans to completely ban ongoing Russian gas imports.

Yet after the European Parliament in December approved the phase-out of gas and oil imports from Russia, experts noted that ending Russian imports alone will not guarantee diversification.

“Diversification cannot mean replacing one dominant supplier with another one,” said Raffaele Piria, senior fellow at Berlin’s Ecologic Institute, in a January policy brief. “Europe needs a clear definition of diversification and a strategy that reflects today’s geopolitical realities.”

EU’s gas about-face in wake of Trump-fueled tensions

More recently, the US president’s threat to impose tariffs on EU countries that don’t comply with his demands to annex Greenland has prompted Brussels to rethink its reliance on US gas.

“In these turbulent geopolitical times, Europe must stand strong and united — and choose independence. That means doubling down on clean, safe, home-grown energy,” said the European Commission’s Energy and Housing commissioner, Dan Jorgensen, at the North Sea Summit in Hamburg in late January.

A “Joint Offshore Wind Investment Pact” was among several measures agreed at the summit aimed at securing independent and clean energy within the EU.

Jorgensen said in January that Europe should also source its gas from countries such as Canada, Qatar and Algeria.

“The future of energy in Europe could be changing route,” noted Jaller-Makarewicz of the shifting headwinds. “If the EU keeps strong on the plans to replace gas consumption by clean energy, then gas demand will decrease.”

In this scenario, existing US LNG deals based on Memorandums of Understanding — formal agreements that are not legally-binding — might collapse, the energy expert explained.

Meanwhile, over 120 European and international civil society groups sent a letter to EU leaders recently urging them to cancel negotiations for the US-EU trade deal that ties Europe to energy imports from across the Atlantic.

title: Cuba Gambles on Green Energy to End Crippling Blackouts | OilPrice.com
description:
Russia has said it will continue to supply Cuba with crude, although this could take some time to be seen.
One obvious solution to Cuba’s energy concerns is the development of the country’s renewable energy resources.
However, the development of its renewable energy capacity has remained limited, until recently, due to a lack of political will and funding.
However, the government published its National Energy Transition Strategy in September 2024, aimed at increasing Cuba’s domestic crude output and developing its renewable energy sources.
Cuba aims to significantly increase its renewable energy capacity in the coming years, to decrease the country’s reliance on foreign imports of fossil fuels and increase its energy security.
text:
Cuba has long been suffering from an energy crisis, with residents facing regular blackouts, which has been exacerbated by the recent United States intervention in Venezuela and the halt of critical energy exports to Cuba. Now, the island nation is assessing a potential future in renewables to diversify its energy mix and boost security.

In February, Cuba’s energy crisis deepened as fuel rationing was implemented, following U.S. President Trump’s campaign to provoke regime change by cutting off the island’s energy supply, most of which came from Venezuela. In January, Trump threatened to impose tariffs on any country that sends oil to Cuba.

Mexico initially continued to supply Cuba with crude, justifying the move as humanitarian aid, although President Sheinbaum halted oil shipments to the island nation in February in response to Trump’s tariff threat. “It’s currently on hold, and we are seeking to avoid any negative impact on Mexico and, as always, to find the best diplomatic solution for Cuba to receive the fuel,” President Sheinbaum stated during a press conference on 9th February. Mexico, instead, sent large quantities of humanitarian aid to Cuba last month.

Russia has said it will continue to supply Cuba with crude, although this could take some time to be seen. Meanwhile, Cubans are turning to charcoal for cooking and to solar panels for electricity generation. The Cuban government is rationing fuel for use in essential services and indispensable economic activities.

As Cuba hurries to find alternative fuel sources to tackle the immediate shortages, a long-term solution must be found to reduce reliance on foreign oil imports and strengthen the country’s energy security. Cuba is highly vulnerable to extreme weather, which makes its dependence on fossil fuels alarming. One obvious solution to Cuba’s energy concerns is the development of the country’s renewable energy resources.

Related: Why Trump Wants Magnets More Than Gold

The Cuban government recently committed to the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC), pledging that the country would increase its renewable energy capacity to 26 percent of the total energy supply by 2035. At present, renewables, mainly solar, contribute just 9 percent of the state electricity utility's supply.

Cuba installed its first solar panels in the late 1980s and opened its first wind farm in 1999. However, the development of its renewable energy capacity has remained limited, until recently, due to a lack of political will and funding. However, the government published its National Energy Transition Strategy in September 2024, aimed at increasing Cuba’s domestic crude output and developing its renewable energy sources.

China has since become a key partner, supporting Cuba financially and providing expertise for the development of the green energy sector. The island’s largest wind farm to date – La Herradura 1 – is currently under construction, funded by Chinese investment. The first stage consists of the installation of 22 wind turbines to generate 33 MW of clean power.

In December 2024, China signed a deal with Cuba to develop seven solar parks with a combined capacity of 35 MW. The Cuban government aims to develop 92 solar parks with a total capacity of 2GW by 2028, supported by Chinese financing. A reported 35 of these solar parks were completed by October 2025.

However, to make these solar parks more reliable, Cuba must invest in battery storage as well as in the country’s long underfunded transmission network. At present, around 16 percent of the electricity produced in Cuba is lost due to failures in transmission.

Investing in battery storage may not be on the cards. Ricardo Torres, an energy economist at the American University in Washington, explained, “The energy transition outlined by the government would require investments of around $8 billion to $10 billion over the next decade… Cuba simply does not have that kind of money, and China will not pay for everything.”

In addition to funding, Cuba will need to provide the necessary technical expertise to manage renewable energy and modern transmission projects, and the country currently lacks skilled workers in this field.

Related: Magnet Wars: How the U.S. Plans to Break China’s Grip on Rare Earths

Despite several hurdles still to overcome to develop Cuba’s large-scale renewable energy capacity, several wealthier households and businessowners are now investing in solar panels. While poorer Cubans are turning to charcoal or wood for energy, an increasing number of solar panels can be seen in wealthier neighbourhoods, particularly in the capital of Havana.

The number of solar panel installation firms has risen significantly since 2024, when the Cuban government relaxed restrictions on importing solar panels, which are mostly produced in China. However, with a small solar package costing around $2,000, most Cubans do not have access to this equipment.

Cuba aims to significantly increase its renewable energy capacity in the coming years, to decrease the country’s reliance on foreign imports of fossil fuels and increase its energy security. However, this will require high levels of funding and expertise, which Cuba will not be able to achieve without international support. Meanwhile, the island nation is facing a severe energy crisis.

By Felicity Bradstock for Oilprice.com

title: US energy chief defends waiver on Russian oil sanctions, blames fear for higher gas prices
description:
By Katharine Jackson and Curtis Williams WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) – Trump administration officials on Sunday defended a decision to temporarily lift some sanctions on Russian oil and predicted that a sharp increase in gasoline prices resulting from the Iran war would last only weeks.
Appearing on multiple TV talk shows, Energy Secretary Chris Wright and ​U.S.
Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz said a waiver issued last week to allow Indian ‌purchases of Russian oil would alleviate pressure on the global market.
Diesel was at $4.33, up 15% ​from a week ago, surging to the highest level since November 2023.
“But it ​won’t be,” Wright said on “Fox News Sunday,” echoing Trump’s prediction that the war will last weeks rather ‌than months.
text:
By Katharine Jackson and Curtis Williams

WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) – Trump administration officials on Sunday defended a decision to temporarily lift some sanctions on Russian oil and predicted that a sharp increase in gasoline prices resulting from the Iran war would last only weeks.

Appearing on multiple TV talk shows, Energy Secretary Chris Wright and ​U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz said a waiver issued last week to allow Indian ‌purchases of Russian oil would alleviate pressure on the global market.

“It’s a 30-day pause to allow, which is just kind of common sense, to allow the millions and millions of barrels of oil that are sitting out on ships to go to Indian refineries,” Waltz said on NBC’s “Meet the Press.”

Wright told CNN’s “State of the Union” that the waiver can help “tamp this fear of shortage of oil, tamp the price spikes and the concerns ‌we see ​in the marketplace.”

With the war now in its second week and no end ⁠in sight, Americans are grappling with higher ⁠prices at the pump, a new complicating factor for the U.S. economy, which unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs in February.

As of Friday, the national average price for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a gallon, up 11% from the previous week and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists group AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, up 15% ​from a week ago, surging to the highest level since November 2023.

“Short term oil prices, which will drop rapidly when the destruction of the Iran nuclear threat is over, is a very small price to pay for U.S.A., and World, Safety ⁠and Peace,” President Donald Trump wrote in a Truth Social post on ⁠Sunday night. “ONLY FOOLS WOULD THINK DIFFERENTLY!”

Earlier on Sunday, Wright said there is no shortage of ​oil or natural gas and blamed price increases on “fear and perception” that the Iran operation will be a drawn-out affair.

“But it ​won’t be,” Wright said on “Fox News Sunday,” echoing Trump’s prediction that the war will last weeks rather ‌than months.

U.S. crude futures surged more than 20% in early trading in Asia on Monday, hitting their highest since July 2022, as the expanding war fueled fears of tighter supply and prolonged disruptions to shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.

In a statement late on Sunday, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer called on Trump to sell oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, which currently ⁠holds about 415 million barrels of oil, or more than the entire world uses in four days.

“Trump should release oil from the SPR now to stabilize markets, bring prices down, and stop the price shock that American families are already feeling thanks to ⁠his reckless war,” said Schumer, a New ‌York Democrat.

The Energy Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Trump on ⁠Thursday told Reuters that he was not looking to tap the strategic reserve.

Senator John Kennedy, ​a Louisiana ‌Republican, criticized energy speculators.

“The oil prices have gone up because you’ve got a bunch ​of oil traders ⁠out there in their Gucci loafers, with their caramel Frappuccinos who are bidding up the price,” Kennedy said on “Fox News Sunday.”

Political analysts say a persistent rise in gasoline prices could hurt Republicans in the November midterm elections when control of the U.S. Congress will be at stake. A Reuters/Ipsos poll last month found that most respondents rejected Trump’s characterization of the economy as “booming.”

(Reporting by Katharine Jackson and Timothy Gardner in Washington, Curtis Williams in Houston and Diana Jones in Chicago; Writing by Steve Holland; Editing by Matthew Lewis, ​Sergio Non and Lincoln Feast.)

name: Detroit American Energy
title: Energy secretary says "period of elevated energy prices" will be temporary - CBS News
description:
Washington — Energy Secretary Chris Wright said Sunday that elevated energy prices amid the escalating war with Iran will be "temporary," stressing he believes gas prices "shouldn't go much higher."
"We have a temporary period of elevated energy prices, but it will not be long," Wright said on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan."
He called that scenario "unacceptable," while noting that the real risk to energy prices "was not doing anything."
It does involve a temporary impediment to energy production, but on the other side, it will allow much more energy production and much lower energy prices."
"We're not helping Russia by just accelerating the sale of their oil to stop the rise of energy prices and keep European and Asian refineries in oil," Wright said Sunday.
text:
Washington — Energy Secretary Chris Wright said Sunday that elevated energy prices amid the escalating war with Iran will be "temporary," stressing he believes gas prices "shouldn't go much higher."

"We have a temporary period of elevated energy prices, but it will not be long," Wright said on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan."

Concerns about a global energy crisis have grown as the war with Iran enters a second week. But Wright claimed "in the worst case, this is weeks — this is not months, and it leads to a much better place."

"It leads to an Iran that's defanged, that can't threaten its neighbors, can't threaten American soldiers, and can't continue to drive up energy prices by making a mess of the Middle East," Wright said. "They can move to commerce, not conflict."

The comments come as the war has continued to push up prices at the pump, with gas prices up 14% in the past week, according to AAA data. The national average rose to $3.45 per gallon Sunday, after dipping below $3 in December. But Wright said gas prices "shouldn't go much higher than they are here, because the world is very well supplied with oil."

"There's no energy shortage at all in the Western Hemisphere, the United States is a net exporter of oil, a large net exporter of natural gas," he said. "But refineries in Asia and Europe are seeing an interruption from the normal crude flows."

Wright cited "massive energy stores around the world," noting that "what you're seeing is emotional reactions and fear that this is a long-term war." He stressed that "this is not a long-term war."

The energy secretary said while previous administrations have "begged, bartered and bribed the Iranian government to stop its nefarious activity," it "simply hasn't worked."

"Now is the time to end their risk to America and the world," Wright said.

Meanwhile, the war has dramatically reduced shipments of oil and liquefied natural gas through the Strait of Hormuz, a waterway through which about 20% of global oil shipments normally travel. Asked by Margaret Brennan about when the strait could see a return to the typical 20 million barrels of oil transported per day, Wright said he expects things will return to that level "relatively soon," though he noted that protection by the U.S. military may be necessary.

"All of our military assets right now are focused on ending Iran's ability to kill their neighbors, threaten American soldiers and threaten ship traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, but that's going swimmingly well," he said. "I think in the relatively near term, you're going to see their capacity so low that we'll see more normal ship traffic return to the Strait of Hormuz."

Wright argued that the operation in Iran will "bring in an era of even lower energy prices, because a major energy producing region of the world, the Middle East, will no longer have a strong, powerful Iran that can threaten their neighbors, that can threaten the United States of America, and was not far away from a nuclear bomb." He called that scenario "unacceptable," while noting that the real risk to energy prices "was not doing anything."

"The president's going to continue to stay focused on ending a 47-year conflict, stay focused on growing the global energy supply," Wright said. "This is actually part of that effort. It does involve a temporary impediment to energy production, but on the other side, it will allow much more energy production and much lower energy prices."

The Trump administration has resisted calls to tap into the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, but Wright said Sunday that "we're more than happy to use that if it's needed." Still, Wright said that oil was needed at refineries in Europe and Asia, and there is more than 100 million barrels of untapped Russian oil that could be used. The U.S. Treasury Department said last week that India can buy crude oil and petroleum products from Russia until April 4, calling the sanctions waiver a "stop-gap measure" to "alleviate pressure" on the market.

"We're not helping Russia by just accelerating the sale of their oil to stop the rise of energy prices and keep European and Asian refineries in oil," Wright said Sunday. "We're just doing pragmatic things to get through a short period."

title: Are we there yet? Readers wants update on renewable energy projects - Yahoo
description:
8—Dear Answer Man: I keep hearing about these big solar and renewable energy projects in and around Olmsted County, but so far the sun is warming us — not powering us — the same as it has been.
Dear Watching, This is an ongoing issue, and Answer Man certainly expects to keep talking about renewable energy for years to come.
According to Clean Energy Economy Minnesota, a nonprofit that follows the energy industry in Minnesota, 53% of Minnesota's energy was powered from "zero-carbon" sources in 2024.
Well, as Brian Todd, news editor, reported back in January, there are basically three projects that are in the works: Byron Solar, Lemon Hill Solar and the two — joint — utility-scale battery projects near Byron.
Both the BESSes and Byron Solar will be connected to the Byron Substation, so that's convenient.
text:
Mar. 8—Dear Answer Man: I keep hearing about these big solar and renewable energy projects in and around Olmsted County, but so far the sun is warming us — not powering us — the same as it has been. When will all this change? — Watching the Sun.

Dear Watching,

This is an ongoing issue, and Answer Man certainly expects to keep talking about renewable energy for years to come. According to Minnesota's clean energy 2040 plan, signed by Gov. Tim Walz in 2023, the state plans to become carbon-free in its electrical production by 2040.

Here in 2026, we are four years — and counting down — to an interim goal of being 80% carbon-free. According to Clean Energy Economy Minnesota, a nonprofit that follows the energy industry in Minnesota, 53% of Minnesota's energy was powered from "zero-carbon" sources in 2024. Of that, 33% was from renewable sources — wind, solar, biomass — and 20% came from nuclear.

And most new electrical production in Minnesota — like 99% — has come from renewables, particularly wind and solar.

So, yes, the general consensus is that the state is behind pretty much any solar or wind project out there, especially if it plans to reduce the number of coal plants and even natural gas plants producing power.

Where does that get us with these local projects? Well, as Brian Todd, news editor, reported back in January, there are basically three projects that are in the works: Byron Solar, Lemon Hill Solar and the two — joint — utility-scale battery projects near Byron.

Let's start with the project that's been around the longest: Byron Solar. Karl Pierce, senior director of development for EDF Power Solutions, said, "We hope to finalize design in the summer to allow us to start construction on the Byron Solar project in the late fall."

According to the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission (PUC), the project, which is expected to generate up to 200 megawatts of power, has only made offloading agreements for 100 megawatts. Where will that other 100 megawatts go?

Well, let's skip to the project that stores power. Perhaps, the side-by-side Crane Storage Project and Sandhill Storage Project — called BESS or battery energy storage systems — once constructed will each store up to 200 megawatts of power that can be returned to the grid when needed. The two have undergone public comment, though few people apparently showed up to ask questions.

According to the PUC, the facilities would be online by late 2028.

Both the BESSes and Byron Solar will be connected to the Byron Substation, so that's convenient.

Finally, there is Lemon Hill Solar, located in Viola and Haverhill townships. In February, the PUC published more public comments from folks who generally are opposed to the project. So, PUC permitting is still required for this project.

With all of this, there's a growing need for electrical power. The state's energy production continues to climb, up 9% in the past five years and 37% since 2001.

Generally speaking, Answer Man would expect to see more of the sun's rays converted to electricity.

Though this time of year, I'm just happy to feel the sun's warmth on my face.

text:
Photo By Dani Johnson | U.S. Navy Student Team is recognized as the Student Team of the Year. The 50th annual...... read more read more

Photo By Dani Johnson | U.S. Navy Student Team is recognized as the Student Team of the Year. The 50th annual Joint Culinary Training Exercise started Feb. 27 at MacLaughlin Fitness Center and continues until March 4. JCTE is about improving readiness of our armed forces. The competing military members hone their skills as culinary arts specialists and improve the quality of food services to their units, enabling them to better perform their duties at home and in a tactical environment. The exercise, administered by the Joint Culinary Training Center, is the largest American Culinary Federation-sanctioned competition in North America and showcased the talent of more than 140 military chefs from five military services around the globe and three international teams - France, United Kingdom, and Germany. see less | View Image Page
